title: Rough bullet notes used to make a concise, professional one-page brief
content:
Convert my rough notes into a clean one-page brief.

Requirements:
- Professional, concise tone. Plain English.
- Keep it to ~250–400 words.
- Preserve facts and numbers exactly; don’t invent details.
- If something is missing or ambiguous, add a short “Open Questions” section instead of guessing.

Output format:
1) Title (max 12 words)
2) Context (1–2 sentences)
3) Key Points (5–8 bullets)
4) Recommendation / Next Step (2–4 bullets)
5) Risks & Dependencies (3–5 bullets)
6) Open Questions (only if needed)

Here are my notes:
[PASTE NOTES HERE]

title: Benefit-First Marketing Copy (Web + Ads)
content:
I’m going to paste a short description of my product and its features. Convert it into benefit-first marketing copy.

Input (paste below):
- Product name:
- Who it’s for (target audience):
- Problem it solves:
- Top features (bullets):
- Price point or positioning (budget/mid/premium):
- Main objection people have:
- Desired action (buy, book a demo, join waitlist, etc.):
- Brand vibe (e.g., playful, minimalist, bold):

Output:
1) One clear value proposition (1 sentence)
2) 5 benefit bullets (no jargon, outcomes over features)
3) A short website hero section:
   - headline (max 8 words)
   - subheadline (1–2 sentences)
   - primary CTA (2–4 words)
   - secondary CTA (2–4 words)
4) 3 ad variations (each: headline + primary text + CTA)
5) 2 social captions (one short, one slightly longer)

Guidelines:
- Keep it friendly and helpful.
- Write like a real person, not a corporate brochure.
- If anything is missing or unclear, make reasonable assumptions and list them at the end under “Assumptions.”

title: Instagram carousel hero: sleek product feature announcement
content:
Create a cohesive 5-slide Instagram carousel (1080×1350 each) for a premium software feature announcement, designed to sit naturally in a dark UI.

Visual direction (keep consistent across all slides):
- Aesthetic: high-end, cool-toned, cinematic, low-key lighting; restrained saturation; subtle blue/gray grade
- Background: minimal, dark charcoal/ink gradient with soft vignette; faint texture like matte paper or brushed metal
- Lighting: directional soft key light from upper-left; gentle rim light; controlled shadows; soft falloff
- Materials: glass + anodized aluminum + matte UI panels; micro-reflections; no glossy plastic look
- Composition: generous negative space; strong grid alignment; modern minimalism; no clutter
- Text: do NOT render readable typography (use abstract text blocks/lines). Leave clear text-safe areas.

Slide-by-slide concepts:
1) Hero slide: floating dark UI card with a single glowing accent line (cool cyan) implying “new feature”; centered, bold presence
2) Benefit slide: two overlapping UI panels with subtle before/after separation; a thin divider glow; minimal icon shapes
3) Proof slide: a compact dashboard-like UI cluster with tiny abstract charts (non-readable) and a soft highlight on one widget
4) How-it-works slide: three connected UI tiles linked by faint luminous lines; clean system flow feel
5) CTA slide: one large UI panel with an empty headline block area and a prominent abstract button shape; strongest contrast here

Camera & render notes:
- Perspective: slight 3/4 angle, 35–50mm feel, mild depth of field; crisp edges with soft background blur
- Add subtle film grain; avoid heavy bloom; keep highlights controlled
- No people, no hands, no office props, no warm daylight, no bright white backgrounds

Output: five separate images with matching lighting and grading, each clearly distinct but part of the same set.

title: Write a modern, direct email that gets a clear decision (with next steps)
content:
Help me write a modern, direct email that gets a clear decision and moves the work forward.

Context
- Audience/recipient: [who I’m emailing + role]
- Relationship: [new / ongoing / vendor / internal]
- Goal (one sentence): [what I need them to decide/do]
- Background (2–4 bullets):
  - [key fact 1]
  - [key fact 2]
  - [constraint/why now]
- Options (if relevant):
  - Option A: [what it is + tradeoff]
  - Option B: [what it is + tradeoff]
- My recommendation: [A/B/other + why]
- Deadline / time sensitivity: [date/time + consequence]
- Links or references to include: [URLs or doc names]

Requirements
- Tone: modern, direct, calm (not stiff, not overly friendly).
- Keep it skimmable: short paragraphs, bullets where helpful.
- Include exactly one clear call-to-action and one explicit deadline.
- If anything is ambiguous, make a reasonable assumption and flag it with “Assumption: …” at the end.

Output
1) Subject line options (5 total):
   - 2 straightforward
   - 2 benefit-oriented
   - 1 ultra-short (<= 4 words)
2) Email body (120–180 words)
3) Closing line that makes the next step effortless (1 sentence)
4) A 1-line follow-up to send if they don’t respond by the deadline

title: From meeting transcript to clear action items and owners
content:
I’m going to paste raw meeting notes/transcript below. Convert it into a clean, business-ready action list.

Rules:
- Only use information present in the notes. If something is missing, write “TBD” and list what’s needed.
- Prefer concrete verbs (Decide, Draft, Review, Send, Implement).
- Keep it tight and skimmable.

Output format:
1) Decisions made (bullets)
2) Action items (table with: Task | Owner | Due date | Priority (H/M/L) | Dependencies | Notes)
3) Open questions / blockers (bullets)
4) Risks flagged (bullets)
5) Next meeting: proposed agenda (3–6 bullets)

Here are the notes:
[PASTE NOTES HERE]

title: From rough idea to a concise screen spec (layout, states, copy)
content:
Convert my rough UI idea into a clean, build-ready screen spec.

Input (paste my notes):
[PASTE NOTES]

Output format:
1) Screen goal (1 sentence)
2) Primary users + context (2–3 bullets)
3) Layout blueprint
- Header
- Main content
- Sidebar/drawer (if any)
- Footer
(Use short bullets; name components; include hierarchy and spacing priorities.)
4) Key components (table)
- Component name | Purpose | Props/variants | Empty state | Error state
5) Interaction & states
- Default
- Loading
- Empty
- Error
- Success
- Disabled
6) Microcopy
- Page title
- Primary CTA
- Secondary CTA
- Form labels/placeholders (if any)
- Validation messages
- Empty-state message
(Keep copy short, consistent, and actionable.)
7) Accessibility & usability checks (5 bullets max)

Constraints:
- Be professional and concise.
- Don’t invent extra features; if something is missing, list it under “Open questions” (max 5).

title: Hero image prompt generator (quick fill-in)
content:
Create an image-generation prompt for a premium, dark-themed product interface.

Ask me for only these 4 inputs (keep it brief):
1) Subject (what’s the main thing?)
2) Setting (where is it?)
3) Style (photoreal / 3D / illustration)
4) Aspect ratio (e.g., 16:9, 1:1, 9:16)

Then output ONE paste-ready prompt that includes:
- Subject + 2–3 defining details (materials, texture, distinguishing features)
- Composition (camera angle, framing, depth of field)
- Lighting (low-key, directional, soft shadows) and mood (calm, premium)
- Color palette (cool/neutral grading: deep navy, charcoal, steel gray; restrained saturation)
- Background (minimal, slightly darkened, subtle atmospheric falloff)
- Quality cues (high detail, clean edges, no clutter)
- A short negative prompt line to avoid: bright daylight, warm cozy lifestyle vibes, busy background, oversaturated colors, text/logos/watermarks

Keep the final prompt under 900 characters and avoid brand names.

Start now by asking for the 4 inputs.

title: Coding desk with neon terminal glow
content: Moody, premium cinematic scene of a modern coding workstation designed to sit inside a dark product interface: a matte black desk with subtle texture, ultra-thin monitor showing a code editor and terminal (no readable brands; code is abstract/placeholder), cool cyan/blue terminal glow reflecting softly on a mechanical keyboard and aluminum laptop, minimal desk objects (one espresso cup, one pen, one small notebook), deep charcoal background with gentle falloff. Composition: 3/4 angle from slightly above desk height, subject centered with negative space on the right for UI overlays, shallow depth of field (keyboard in focus, background bokeh). Lighting: low-key, single soft key light from screen + faint rim light from left, soft shadows, restrained contrast, no warm sunlight. Color grading: cool neutrals, deep navy accents, desaturated, high-end tech aesthetic. Style: photorealistic, crisp detail, clean lines, no clutter, no people, no logos, no bright whites. Camera look: 35mm lens, f/2.0, ISO 400, subtle film grain, high dynamic range, 4k.

title: Ruthless 60-minute action plan
content:
Take my raw to-do list below and turn it into a practical plan I can start immediately.

Rules:
- Only plan for the next 60 minutes.
- Pick at most 5 tasks total.
- Choose the smallest next actions (not vague projects).
- Include a quick “parking lot” for everything you’re not choosing.
- If anything is missing, make a reasonable assumption and keep going.

Output format:
1) The 60-minute plan (minute-by-minute or timeboxed blocks)
2) The 5 chosen tasks with one-sentence “definition of done” each
3) Parking lot (bullets)
4) One sentence telling me what to do first (no pep talk)

My to-do list:
[paste here]

Context (optional):
- Current time:
- Energy level (low/medium/high):
- Hard stops / meetings:
- Tools available (laptop/phone/etc.):

title: Evidence-first research brief with source table, claims audit, and next steps
content:
Help me research the topic below and produce an evidence-first brief I can use to make decisions.

TOPIC:
[Paste the question/problem and any constraints]

AUDIENCE & DECISION:
- Who will use this and what decision are they trying to make?
- What counts as “good enough” evidence (timeframe, geography, industry, sample size, etc.)?

SCOPE:
- Include:
- Exclude:
- Time window:
- Preferred sources (if any):

OUTPUT REQUIREMENTS (follow this structure exactly)
1) Executive answer (6–10 bullet points)
- Lead with what we can say confidently.
- Separate: strong evidence vs. plausible but uncertain vs. unknown.

2) Key definitions & assumptions
- Define the terms that could change the conclusion.
- List assumptions you’re making because of missing info.

3) Claims audit table
Create a table with columns:
- Claim (atomic, testable)
- Status (Supported / Partially supported / Unclear / Contradicted)
- Evidence summary (1–2 sentences)
- Best source(s) (citation + link)
- Evidence strength (High/Med/Low) with justification
- Notes / caveats

4) Source map
- 8–15 sources total, prioritized.
- Group by type: peer-reviewed research, government/NGO, industry reports, reputable journalism, primary data.
- For each: what it’s good for, what it’s not good for, and any bias/conflict-of-interest signals.

5) What would change the conclusion?
- 5–8 “tripwires”: new data points or findings that would materially alter the recommendation.

6) Practical next steps
- A 2-week research plan: what to validate first, what to ignore, and where to look.
- If relevant, propose a simple experiment or measurement plan.

PROCESS RULES
- Ask up to 5 clarifying questions only if essential; otherwise proceed with stated assumptions.
- Prefer primary sources and meta-analyses over opinion pieces.
- Quote key numbers and include units, denominators, and timeframes.
- Avoid hallucinated citations. If you’re not sure a source exists, say so and suggest where to verify.
- Write clearly and practically; no fluff.

Start by listing your clarifying questions (if any), then deliver the brief.

title: Benefit-Driven Landing Page Copy (Concise, High-Conversion)
content:
Write concise landing page copy for the product described below. Keep it professional, direct, and skimmable. Avoid hype, clichés, and exclamation points.

INPUT
- Product name:
- One-sentence description:
- Target audience (role + industry):
- Top 5 features (bullets):
- Primary pain point it solves:
- Key differentiator vs competitors:
- Proof available (metrics, testimonials, logos, case study) or “none”:
- Primary CTA (e.g., Start free trial, Book a demo):
- Objections to address (up to 3):
- Compliance constraints / banned claims:

OUTPUT (in this exact order)
1) Hero section
- Headline (max 8 words)
- Subheadline (max 20 words)
- 3 benefit bullets (each max 10 words)
- CTA button text (2–4 words)

2) Problem → Solution block
- 2 short sentences describing the problem
- 2 short sentences describing how the product solves it

3) How it works (3 steps)
- Step name (2–4 words) + one sentence each

4) Value props (6 bullets)
- Each bullet: Benefit first, then feature in parentheses

5) Social proof
- If proof provided: write 2 tight proof bullets using the inputs
- If none: write 2 “trust builders” that don’t invent numbers (e.g., security, support, onboarding)

6) Objection handling
- For each objection: one sentence response, one sentence reassurance

7) Closing CTA
- One sentence outcome-focused line (max 18 words)
- Repeat CTA button text

STYLE RULES
- Use second person (“you”).
- Prefer concrete verbs and outcomes.
- Replace vague claims with specifics from inputs.
- If any input is missing, make the best neutral assumption and don’t fabricate metrics.

title: From rough idea to a clear product spec + design tasks
content:
I have a rough UI/product idea and I want you to turn it into a clear, buildable design spec.

Context
- Product/app: [what it is]
- Target users: [who]
- Primary job-to-be-done: [what they’re trying to accomplish]
- Platform(s): [web / iOS / Android / desktop]
- Constraints: [timeline, tech, accessibility, brand, legal, performance]
- What I already have (optional): [notes, sketches, existing screens, competitor links]

My rough idea (paste as-is)
[PASTE HERE]

What I need from you
1) One-paragraph “north star” description of the feature that a designer + engineer will interpret the same way.
2) Assumptions you’re making (bullet list) and 5–10 focused questions only if they unblock major decisions.
3) User stories in the format: As a [user], I want [goal], so that [benefit]. Group by primary vs secondary users.
4) Key user flows:
   - Happy path (step-by-step)
   - 2–3 critical edge cases/failure states
   - Entry points and exit points
5) Information architecture: list the screens/views and what lives on each.
6) Wireframe-level layout guidance for the main screen(s): describe the structure (header, primary actions, content regions, empty/loading/error states). No visual styling yet—just layout.
7) Interaction details: state changes, validation rules, micro-interactions, and keyboard/navigation behavior.
8) Accessibility checklist tailored to this feature (WCAG-minded, practical).
9) Analytics/instrumentation: events to track + properties (name them clearly).
10) A prioritized task list:
   - Design tasks (research, IA, wireframes, prototyping, usability test)
   - Engineering tasks (API needs, data model, edge cases, QA)
   - Include a suggested order and what can be parallelized.

Output format
- Use clear headings.
- Keep it concise but complete (avoid fluffy text).
- When you need to choose between options, present 2 viable approaches with pros/cons and a recommendation.

If something is ambiguous, prefer making a reasonable assumption and flagging it rather than stalling.

title: Hero image of a product screenshot on a floating device
content:
Create a premium, cinematic hero image for a dark-mode product interface.

Subject: a sleek floating device (choose one: thin laptop / tablet / ultrawide monitor) displaying a crisp screenshot of [APP/PRODUCT]’s dark UI (no readable brand names; UI elements should be clean and believable). The device is angled 3/4 view, slightly above eye level, with subtle perspective and a thin bezel.

Scene & background: minimal studio setup with a deep charcoal-to-midnight gradient backdrop, faint volumetric haze, and soft bokeh specks kept very subtle. No bright white environments.

Lighting: low-key, directional key light from upper left with soft falloff; gentle rim light on the device edges; controlled reflections on glass; soft shadow under the floating device (suggesting depth). Avoid harsh glare.

Color & mood: restrained saturation, cool-neutral grading (deep navies, gunmetal grays, cool whites). High-end, calm, confident mood suitable for a dark product interface.

Composition: centered with generous negative space for headline copy on [LEFT/RIGHT]; keep the UI legible but not text-focused. Depth separation between device and background.

Detail cues: premium materials (anodized aluminum, matte finish), micro-scratches only if subtle, clean typography shapes, crisp icons, realistic screen bloom kept minimal.

Output style: photorealistic CGI or hyper-real studio product photography look, sharp focus on the device and UI, slightly softer background, 16:9.

Optional variants: include [SECONDARY ELEMENT] as a faint floating card behind the main device (e.g., a chart panel, settings modal) matching the same UI style and lighting.

title: Turn one long idea into a 10-post social thread (hooks + CTAs)
content:
Take the long content below and turn it into a 10-post thread for [PLATFORM]. Keep it modern, direct, and easy to skim.

Input long content:
---
[PASTE YOUR LONG CONTENT HERE]
---

Requirements:
- Write exactly 10 posts.
- Each post: 1–2 short sentences (max 280 characters each).
- Post 1: sharp hook + clear promise (no fluff).
- Posts 2–8: one idea per post, in logical order, with concrete takeaways.
- Post 9: quick recap in 3 tight bullets.
- Post 10: CTA that invites replies (ask 1 specific question) + soft plug to [LINK/OFFER] without sounding salesy.
- If the long content has stats/examples, include the best 2 and keep numbers intact.
- Remove filler, hedging, and buzzwords. Use active voice.
- If something is unclear in the source, make a reasonable assumption and keep going (don’t ask questions).

Output format:
1) ...
2) ...
...
10) ...

title: Transform a messy feature idea into a simple UI spec + wireframe checklist
content:
Take the feature idea below and convert it into a practical UI design spec I can hand to a designer (or build myself).

Feature idea (paste):
[FEATURE IDEA]

Context:
- Product type: [WEB/IOS/ANDROID/DESKTOP]
- Primary user: [WHO]
- Goal/outcome: [WHAT SUCCESS LOOKS LIKE]
- Constraints: [TIME/TECH/LEGAL/BRAND/ACCESSIBILITY]

Output (keep it concise):
1) One-sentence problem statement
2) Primary user story + 3 acceptance criteria
3) Key screens/components (bulleted)
4) Happy-path flow (step-by-step)
5) Empty/loading/error states to design
6) Data needs: fields required, validation rules, defaults
7) Interaction notes: keyboard behavior, focus order, microcopy guidelines (short)
8) Accessibility checklist: contrast, labels, hit targets, motion
9) Analytics events (3–6) with event names + properties
10) Open questions / assumptions

Rules:
- Avoid jargon; use plain UI language.
- Prefer simple patterns and minimal UI.
- If details are missing, make reasonable assumptions but list them in #10.

title: Design critique + next-iteration plan for a single screen
content:
Act as a senior product designer. Critique my screen and give me a clear plan for the next iteration.

Context
- Product: [what it is]
- Platform: [iOS/Android/Web/Desktop]
- Primary user: [who]
- Primary job-to-be-done: [what they’re trying to accomplish]
- Screen name: [e.g., Checkout, Settings]
- Traffic source/state: [first-time/returning, empty/error/loading]
- Constraints: [brand rules, accessibility level, tech limits, timeline]

Screen description (paste text notes or a rough layout)
[Describe layout top-to-bottom: header, content sections, CTAs, navigation, key components, copy snippets]

What I want from you
1) Quick read (5 bullets): what works, what’s risky, what’s unclear.
2) UX issues: identify friction, missing info, confusing hierarchy, cognitive load, and likely user errors.
3) Visual hierarchy & layout: spacing, alignment, grouping, scanning paths, emphasis, and consistency.
4) Copy & microcopy: rewrite key labels/messages/CTAs in the same voice; note where tone or specificity is off.
5) Accessibility: contrast, type size, tap targets, focus order, error handling, and state visibility. Call out WCAG-related concerns.
6) Decision log: list the top 3 design decisions I should make (with options + pros/cons + your recommendation).
7) Next iteration plan: a prioritized checklist (P0/P1/P2) with concrete changes.

Output format
- Use headings and bullet points.
- Be direct and specific—no generic advice.
- If something is ambiguous, list up to 5 clarifying questions at the end (only if needed).

title: Product hero image prompt
content:
Create an image-generation prompt for a premium, dark UI product hero image.

Subject: [what the product/scene is] (single clear focal object)
Setting: minimal studio or abstract environment that won’t clash with a dark interface
Mood: sleek, calm, high-end, slightly mysterious
Color: cool/neutral grade (deep navy, charcoal, graphite, muted steel), restrained saturation
Lighting: low-key, directional key light + subtle rim light, soft shadows, gentle falloff, controlled specular highlights
Composition: centered or rule-of-thirds, plenty of negative space for UI copy, clean silhouette, depth separation from background
Camera: 50mm or 85mm look, shallow depth of field, crisp focus on the subject, background softly blurred
Materials: realistic textures (anodized metal, matte glass, soft-touch plastic, fine fabric) with believable micro-detail
Background: dark gradient or subtle haze, no bright white surfaces, no warm sunny daylight

Output format: Return a single ready-to-paste prompt that includes:
- the full scene description
- a short “style line” (cinematic, premium, cool grade)
- optional negatives: “no text, no watermark, no clutter, no oversaturation, no harsh daylight”

Now generate the final prompt for: [YOUR SUBJECT HERE].

title: Social media hero image for a product or app launch
content:
Create a premium, cinematic social media hero image for a {product/app name} launch.

Subject & scene
- Hero subject: {what we’re showcasing: phone screen / device / product / person}
- Setting: minimal, dark studio environment with subtle texture (matte charcoal, deep navy, or graphite)
- Add 1–2 supporting props max: {prop ideas: card, pen, headset, small box}, kept understated

Composition
- Aspect ratio: {1:1 or 4:5 or 16:9}
- Center-weighted or rule-of-thirds; strong negative space for overlay text on {left/right/top}
- Clean silhouette, clear separation from background via rim light
- If screen is visible: show an abstract UI (no readable text), high contrast, crisp reflections

Lighting & mood
- Low-key lighting with a single soft key light from {left/right} at ~45°
- Subtle cool rim light (blue/steel) and soft shadow falloff
- Controlled specular highlights on edges; no blown whites
- Mood: confident, modern, restrained; high-end dark-interface aesthetic

Color & finish
- Color palette: cool neutrals (graphite, slate, deep navy) with one accent color: {accent hex or color}
- Slight filmic contrast, mild grain, clean but not glossy-plastic
- Avoid warm daylight, cozy lifestyle tones, bright white backgrounds, and oversaturated colors

Camera & style
- Camera angle: {eye-level / 3/4 angle / top-down}
- Lens look: {35mm / 50mm / 85mm}, shallow depth of field, sharp focal plane on the hero subject
- Ultra-detailed, photoreal or high-end 3D render (choose one): {photoreal / 3D}

Deliverable
- One final image with a dark, minimal background and deliberate negative space for social copy.
- No logos, no watermarks, no readable text.

Optional variants (generate 3 if supported)
- Variant A: tighter crop, stronger rim light
- Variant B: slightly wider, more negative space
- Variant C: same scene with a different accent color: {accent 2}

title: A Clear 5-Step Action Plan
content:
Take the rough idea below and turn it into a practical plan I can execute this week.

Idea (paste):
[YOUR IDEA]

Output requirements:
- Start with a 1–2 sentence “What we’re doing + why it matters.”
- List exactly 5 steps in order (each step starts with a verb).
- For each step: include the goal, the estimated time, and what “done” looks like.
- Call out the single biggest risk and how to reduce it.
- End with the first action I should take in the next 10 minutes.

Constraints:
- Keep it modern, direct, and free of fluff.
- If the idea is too vague, make reasonable assumptions and state them in one short line.

title: Storyboard + Shot Planner That Outputs a Production-Ready Shotlist (JSON + Table)
content:
Build a storyboard and shot plan for the project described below. Your output must be usable by a production team (director, editor, motion designer) and by an image/video generation pipeline.

PROJECT INPUT
- Working title:
- Format: (e.g., 30–90s video, 6-panel storyboard, 12-shot sequence)
- Platform(s):
- Aspect ratio(s):
- Target duration (if video):
- Audience + goal (what should the viewer think/feel/do?):
- Core message (1 sentence):
- Key beats (bullets):
- Constraints (budget/time/locations/brand/legal):
- Must-include assets (logos, UI screens, product shots, characters):
- Must-avoid (visuals/claims/tone):
- References (links or described vibes):

RULES
- Do NOT write a script or polished narrative paragraphs.
- Prioritize shot planning, coverage, and asset orchestration.
- Every shot must have a clear purpose and a reason it exists.
- Ensure continuity: wardrobe/props, screen direction, time of day, color story, spatial logic.
- Include accessibility considerations: legible text, safe motion, captions space, contrast.
- If info is missing, make the smallest reasonable assumptions and list them under “Assumptions”. Do not ask questions unless absolutely blocking.

DELIVERABLES
1) ASSUMPTIONS (only if needed)
- Bullet any assumptions you made so a producer can confirm.

2) STORY STRUCTURE (non-prose)
- Provide a beat grid with: Beat name | Goal | What changes | Stakes | Visual motif | Audio approach.

3) MASTER SHOTLIST (table)
Provide a table with one row per shot using these columns:
- Shot ID (S01, S02…)
- Timestamp / Duration (e.g., 00:00–00:03)
- Scene / Location
- Shot type (WS/MS/CU/ECU/OTS/POV/etc.)
- Camera (angle, lens feel, movement, stabilization)
- Composition (framing, subject placement, negative space for text)
- Action (what happens on screen)
- Intent (why this shot exists)
- Key props / wardrobe / set dressing
- Lighting (direction, contrast, practicals)
- Color grade notes (palette, mood)
- On-screen text (exact text + placement)
- VFX / Motion (callouts, transitions)
- Audio (SFX, VO cue, music beat)
- Continuity notes (what must match previous/next)
- Generative prompt stub (one concise prompt line for image/video gen)

4) ASSET ORCHESTRATION
- Asset list grouped by: Locations, Props, Wardrobe, Graphics/UI, Stock, 3D, Sound.
- For each asset: Owner (role), Source (create/buy/stock), Priority (P0/P1/P2), Reuse shots (IDs).

5) COVERAGE + EDIT PLAN
- Identify: A-roll, B-roll, inserts, transitions, safety shots.
- Recommend edit rhythm (fast/medium/slow) by segment.
- Provide 3 alternative transitions that keep continuity (no generic “fade”).

6) RISK CHECKS
- List top 8 failure modes (e.g., message unclear, pacing, legal claims, continuity breaks, unreadable UI, platform safe zones).
- For each: detection method + mitigation.

7) MACHINE-READABLE JSON
Output a JSON object with:
- project_meta
- assumptions
- beats[]
- shots[] (one object per shot with fields mirroring the shotlist columns)
- assets[]
- risks[]

STYLE
- Clear, practical, production language.
- Prefer specifics over adjectives.
- Keep it concise but complete.

Now create the storyboard + shot plan based on the PROJECT INPUT above.
